summary: This cluster investigates the role of mycosporine-like amino acids (MAAs) as natural photoprotective compounds in marine macroalgae, particularly in response to increased solar UV-B radiation from stratospheric ozone depletion. Seven specific MAAs—including mycosporine-glycine, shinorine, porphyra-334, palythine, asterina-330, palythene, and usujirene—were identified, with absorption spanning both UV-B and UV-A wavelengths. Research focused on Antarctic rhodophytes demonstrated that species with higher MAA concentrations exhibited greater tolerance to natural and artificial UV radiation, establishing a direct correlation between UV-protective compound levels and UV sensitivity. Experimental evidence further showed that MAA formation is regulated by both UV and photosynthetically active radiation (PAR), indicating a complex physiological role beyond simple UV screening.
entity_sample: Davies Reef, Great Barrier Reef, Australia
